county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Courthouse records intact from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Whitman County in 1883 during the territorial period. Named after President John Adams. Primarily agricultural (dryland wheat farming) in the Palouse region. County seat established at Ritzville, a railroad town on the Northern Pacific line.
county_metadata.research_tips: Adams County is one of Washington's prime dryland wheat farming counties, and its genealogical records reflect waves of German-Russian settlers who arrived via the Northern Pacific Railroad in the 1880s-1890s. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in Cheney holds Adams County court and naturalization records. German-Russian families often appear in both Adams and neighboring Lincoln and Grant counties as they homesteaded across the channeled scablands. Check Northern Pacific Railroad land sale records at the Minnesota Historical Society for original purchases. The Ritzville Public Library maintains a local history collection with pioneer family files. Adams County Auditor has deeds from 1883. FamilySearch has microfilmed early marriage records. The county's German-Russian heritage is documented in the collection at Washington State University in Pullman.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from formation; index available in office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes homestead patents and railroad land purchases.

source_profiles.repository: Adams County Auditor / WA Digital Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and certificates from county formation. Searchable through Washington Digital Archives.
Federal census 1890 (destroyed), 1900-1950. Pre-1883 look under Whitman County. Many German-Russian surnames appear.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years. 1890 census destroyed.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal homestead patents in the dryland wheat region. Many filed by German-Russian immigrants.

source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives Eastern Branch, Cheney
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention and petitions filed by German-Russian and Scandinavian immigrants.
Local newspaper covering legal notices, land sales, obituaries, and community news in Adams County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — some issues on microfilm at Washington State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. County records well preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Garfield County in 1883. Named from the Nez Perce word 'Hasotain' meaning 'eel creek.' Located at the confluence of the Snake and Grande Ronde rivers in southeastern Washington. Clarkston is the largest city, forming a twin city with Lewiston, Idaho.
county_metadata.research_tips: Asotin County sits at the Washington-Idaho border and many families had connections across the Snake River to Nez Perce County, Idaho. Researchers should check both states for related records. The Nez Perce tribe has deep roots here — the tribe's 1855 and 1863 treaties directly affected land availability in Asotin County. The Asotin County Museum in Clarkston houses local history materials including pioneer photographs and oral histories. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in Cheney holds court records and naturalization papers. Early settlers came up the Snake River by steamboat, and steamboat passenger manifests at the Oregon Historical Society can document arrivals. The Lewis-Clark Valley was a transit point for miners heading to Idaho gold fields in the 1860s. Check FamilySearch for microfilmed Asotin County vital records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor records from 1883; in-person access primarily

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from formation. Includes early ranching and homestead transactions.
Marriage records from county formation. Many early marriages involved cross-border families with Nez Perce County, Idaho.

source_profiles.source_name: Asotin County Superior Court — Probate Records
source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives Eastern Branch, Cheney
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate case files, wills, and estate inventories from county formation.
BIA records for the Nez Perce tribe including census rolls, allotment records, and treaty-related documents affecting Asotin County lands.
Federal census from 1890 (destroyed) through 1950. Pre-1883 enumerated under Garfield County.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from parts of Yakima and Klickitat counties in 1905. Named after Senator Thomas Hart Benton of Missouri. The county includes the Tri-Cities area (Richland, Kennewick, West Richland). Hanford nuclear site transformed the county during WWII. Pre-Hanford communities of White Bluffs and Hanford were evacuated in 1943.
county_metadata.research_tips: Benton County's history divides into pre-Hanford and post-Hanford eras. The federal government's 1943 acquisition of land for the Hanford nuclear project displaced entire communities including White Bluffs and Hanford town. Records of displaced families may be found in Atomic Energy Commission relocation files at NARA Seattle and the DOE Hanford Reading Room in Richland. Before 1905, research Benton County areas under Yakima or Klickitat counties. The Benton County Historical Society in Prosser maintains records of pre-Hanford farm families. Irrigation from the Yakima Project (Bureau of Reclamation) beginning in the early 1900s brought agricultural settlement. The Central Regional Branch of the State Archives in Ellensburg holds Benton County records. Many Hanford workers came from across the nation during WWII — their personnel records are at DOE and NARA.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Auditor deed records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes records of Hanford-area land condemnations in 1943.

source_profiles.source_name: Hanford Nuclear Reservation Personnel and Community Records
source_profiles.repository: DOE Hanford Reading Room / NARA Seattle
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Personnel records, community relocation files, and construction workforce documentation from the Manhattan Project and Cold War era at Hanford.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — DOE and NARA hold extensive records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Pre-1905 marriages in this area filed under Yakima or Klickitat counties.
Federal census from 1910 (first after county formation). Pre-1905 area enumerated under Yakima/Klickitat.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete. Note dramatic population surge in 1940s from Hanford project.

source_profiles.source_name: Bureau of Reclamation — Yakima Irrigation Project Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Water rights allotments and irrigation project records that brought settlers to the Benton County area.
Local newspapers from Prosser, Kennewick, and Richland. Wartime editions document Hanford-era community changes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Washington State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Some early records sparse due to remote mountain terrain.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Kittitas and Okanogan counties in 1899. Named for Lake Chelan, from a Salish word meaning 'deep water.' Wenatchee became the county seat and grew as the apple capital of the world due to extensive irrigation orchards.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chelan County is the heart of Washington's apple-growing region, and many early settlers came specifically to develop orchards after irrigation projects in the early 1900s. The Chelan County Historical Society (Wenatchee) maintains pioneer files and photograph collections. Before county formation in 1899, check records under Kittitas or Okanogan counties. The Central Regional Branch of the State Archives in Ellensburg holds Chelan County records. The Great Northern Railway brought settlers through Stevens Pass — check railroad employment records at the Minnesota Historical Society. The Wenatchee National Forest has records of homesteaders and timber claim applicants in the upper Chelan and Wenatchee valleys. FamilySearch has microfilmed Chelan County marriage records. Leavenworth was a railroad town before its later Bavarian-themed transformation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Auditor deed records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes orchard land sales and irrigation district records.
Marriage records from county formation. Indexed through Digital Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM Homestead and Timber Culture Patents — Chelan County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents including homestead and timber culture entries in the Chelan and Wenatchee valleys.
Federal census from 1900 (first after county formation). Pre-1899 look under Kittitas or Okanogan.
State death records from 1907 registration. Includes orchard workers and railroad laborers.
Major regional newspaper covering Chelan County. Obituaries, legal notices, orchard industry news.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Some early territorial records incomplete due to sparse settlement.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Washington Territory's original counties created in 1854. Named from the S'Klallam (Clallam) tribe. Located on the northern Olympic Peninsula bordering the Strait of Juan de Fuca and Canada. Port Angeles became the county seat. Olympic National Park covers much of the southern portion.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clallam County on the Olympic Peninsula was one of Washington Territory's earliest organized counties and has records from the 1850s. The S'Klallam tribe (Jamestown, Port Gamble, and Lower Elwha bands) has continuous presence, and tribal enrollment records are at NARA Seattle. The port at Port Angeles was a customs entry point for goods and people crossing from British Columbia — check US Customs passenger manifests at NARA Seattle. Timber companies dominated the economy, and Merrill & Ring, Crown Zellerbach, and other lumber company employment records can identify workers not in census records. The Clallam County Historical Society has pioneer family files and photograph collections. The North Olympic Library System maintains a local history room. Donation Land Claims from the 1850s are at the BLM General Land Office. Canadian-born settlers frequently crossed the Strait — check British Columbia vital records for family connections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records; early territorial records available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from early territorial period. Includes timber company land transactions.
Donation Land Claims from earliest territorial settlement. Names wives of claimants.
BIA records for Jamestown S'Klallam, Lower Elwha Klallam, and Port Gamble S'Klallam communities. Census rolls, allotment records.

source_profiles.source_name: Port Angeles Customs Records — Cross-Strait Arrivals
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Customs manifests for arrivals from Victoria and other British Columbia ports. Documents settlers crossing from Canada.
Marriage records from territorial era. Indexed through Digital Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Port Angeles Evening News and Peninsula Papers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Olympic Peninsula newspapers covering timber industry, fishing, and community life.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Washington State Library.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original Oregon Territory county (created 1845 as Vancouver District)
county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Some early Oregon Territory records are at the Oregon State Archives.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the oldest counties in Washington, originally created as part of Oregon Territory in 1845. Fort Vancouver was the Hudson's Bay Company headquarters for the Columbia District. The county predates Washington Territory (1853) and has the longest continuous record-keeping in the state. Named after William Clark of the Lewis and Clark Expedition.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clark County has the deepest historical records in Washington state due to Fort Vancouver and the Hudson's Bay Company presence dating to the 1820s. HBC employee records are at the Hudson's Bay Company Archives in Winnipeg, Manitoba, and detail fur traders, engages, and their mixed-heritage families. Fort Vancouver National Historic Site maintains research files on military personnel and civilian employees. The county was part of Oregon Territory until 1853, so early records may be at Oregon State Archives. Clark County has Donation Land Claims from 1850 — these are critical because they name wives. Vancouver Barracks military records (1849-1946) at NARA document one of the oldest Army posts west of the Rockies. The Clark County Historical Museum has pioneer family files. The county's proximity to Portland means many families appear in both Oregon and Washington records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording division; records from territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from pre-territorial era. Includes Donation Land Claims and early Fort Vancouver transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — among oldest in Washington state.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Among the earliest DLCs in Washington. Critical because they name wives of claimants. Many claims around Fort Vancouver.
Post returns, personnel records, and quartermaster files from one of the oldest Army posts west of the Rockies.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good at NARA. Fort Vancouver NHS has supplementary files.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from pre-territorial period. Among the earliest in Washington.
Federal census from 1850 (Oregon Territory). Enumerated under Oregon Territory 1850-1860. Look for HBC employees and their families.

source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Seattle Archives / Oregon Province Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic mission baptism, marriage, and burial records from the HBC era. Include mixed-heritage families and fur trade community.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — FamilySearch has microfilmed some collections.

source_profiles.source_name: Vancouver Independent and Clark County Papers
source_profiles.repository: Chronicling America / Clark County Historical Museum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Local newspapers from Vancouver and Clark County.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records well preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Walla Walla County in 1875. Named for the Columbia River. One of Washington's smallest counties by population. Dayton is the county seat and home to the oldest existing courthouse in Washington (1887) and the oldest railroad depot in the state.
county_metadata.research_tips: Columbia County is one of Washington's least populated counties and its records are correspondingly compact, making thorough research feasible. The 1887 Dayton courthouse — the oldest standing courthouse in Washington — has continuously housed records. Before 1875, check records under Walla Walla County. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in Cheney holds court and naturalization records. Early settlers were drawn by the stock-raising potential of the Blue Mountains foothills. The Dayton Historic Depot (1881) documents the Oregon Railroad and Navigation Company line that connected the county. The Columbia County Museum maintains local history files. The county's wheat farming heritage is well documented in agricultural census schedules. FamilySearch has microfilmed early vital records from the county.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor records from formation; in-person access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation housed in Washington's oldest standing courthouse.
Marriage records from county formation. Small population makes comprehensive research feasible.
Federal census from 1880. Pre-1875 enumerated under Walla Walla County.

source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives Eastern Branch, Cheney
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records including wills and estate inventories from ranching and farming families.
Federal homestead patents in the Blue Mountains foothills.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Some early territorial records incomplete.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created as one of Washington Territory's original counties in 1854 from part of Lewis County. Named for the Cowlitz tribe. The Cowlitz River and Columbia River define the county. Longview was a planned city built by Long-Bell Lumber Company in 1923. Mount St. Helens erupted in 1980 devastating parts of eastern Cowlitz County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cowlitz County's genealogical landscape is shaped by the timber industry and the Cowlitz Landing portage route. The Cowlitz Landing (Toledo area) was a key transit point between Puget Sound and the Columbia River from the 1840s — HBC records and early Catholic mission records from St. Francis Xavier mission document families using this route. The Long-Bell Lumber Company built the planned city of Longview in 1923, and company records at the Longview Public Library and Oregon Historical Society document thousands of recruited workers. The 1980 Mount St. Helens eruption destroyed some backcountry records and landmarks. The Cowlitz County Historical Museum in Kelso has pioneer family files and a strong photograph collection. FamilySearch has microfilmed early county records. County Auditor has deeds from the 1850s. The Cowlitz Indian Tribe — federally recognized in 2002 — has historical records relevant to families in the area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording records from territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Includes timber company land transactions and Long-Bell company town deeds.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Francis Xavier Mission Records — Cowlitz
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Seattle Archives / Oregon Province Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic mission records from the Cowlitz Prairie mission including baptisms, marriages, and burials for both settler and Native American families.
Marriage records from territorial era. Indexed through Digital Archives.
Federal census from 1860. Note population boom in 1920s-1930s from Longview timber operations.
DLCs from Cowlitz Prairie settlement. Names wives. Some of earliest claims in Washington.
Longview Daily News and Kelso papers. Strong timber industry coverage.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Lincoln County in 1883. Named after Stephen A. Douglas, U.S. Senator and presidential candidate. Located in central Washington on the Columbia Plateau. Grand Coulee Dam (1942) and the Columbia Basin Irrigation Project transformed the eastern part of the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Douglas County's settlement history centers on dryland wheat farming on the Columbia Plateau, with a major shift when Grand Coulee Dam and the Columbia Basin Project brought irrigation. Before county formation in 1883, check records under Lincoln County. The Central Regional Branch of the State Archives in Ellensburg holds Douglas County records. The Douglas County Historical Museum in Waterville has pioneer family files and early ranch records. Many settlers arrived via the Great Northern Railway — check railroad land sale records. Waterville, the county seat, sits on a high plateau at 2,600 feet and was reached by wagon road from the Columbia River. Bridgeport at the confluence of the Columbia and Okanogan rivers served as a trading post. Rock Island Dam records at Chelan County PUD may document workers. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Douglas County records including marriages.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes dryland wheat farm transactions.
Marriage records from county formation. Indexed through Digital Archives.
Federal census from 1890 (destroyed). Pre-1883 check under Lincoln County.
Federal homestead patents on the Columbia Plateau.
Local newspaper covering Douglas County farming community news, legal notices, and obituaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — microfilm at Washington State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses, though early records are sparse due to small population.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Stevens County in 1899. Named after Elisha P. Ferry, Washington's first state governor. Located in northeastern Washington along the Canadian border. The Colville Indian Reservation covers the western half of the county. Republic was a gold mining town.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ferry County is split between the Colville Indian Reservation (western half) and non-reservation lands centered on the mining town of Republic. The gold rush at Republic (1896) brought miners from across the West — check mining claim records at the BLM and Republic Mining District records. The Colville Confederated Tribes include 12 bands, and tribal records at the Colville Agency (NARA Seattle) cover families across the reservation. Before 1899, check records under Stevens County. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in Cheney holds Ferry County records. The Ferry County Historical Society in Republic has mining-era photographs and family files. The county's small population makes comprehensive research manageable. Many families crossed back and forth from British Columbia — check Canadian records. Republic's Historic District and its fossil beds attract researchers to the area who can also access county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor records from formation; in-person access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes mining claims and homestead entries.
BIA records for the Colville Confederated Tribes covering 12 bands. Census rolls, allotment records, annuity payments, and agency correspondence.
Marriage records from county formation. Small population makes thorough review practical.
Federal census from 1900. Pre-1899 enumerated under Stevens County. Colville Reservation enumerated separately.
Mining claim records, court disputes, and partnership filings from the Republic gold mining district.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — BLM has mining claims; court records at Eastern Branch.
